questions.question_text: Does exposure to natural environments improve directed attention in adults?
accept_indicators: Human participants are exposed to natural environments, green views, vegetation, or biophilic conditions.
The paper contains a direct attention outcome, attention task, or clearly cognate executive-attention measure.
The natural-exposure construct is not merely decorative preference but part of the explanatory or measured condition.
reject_indicators: The paper is about ADHD treatment or attention-deficit symptom management rather than ordinary environmental cognition.
The paper concerns nature exposure but contains no attention outcome and no plausible attention proxy.
The paper is purely preference, aesthetics, or landscape appreciation without cognitive evidence.
edge_case_indicators: The paper studies nature exposure and stress or mood recovery but lacks a direct attention task.
The paper uses restoration language that may imply attention recovery but does not measure it clearly.
The paper is a review or theory paper that is topically relevant but not direct evidence.
questions.llm_panel_summary: This constitution was drafted as an LLM-assisted panel brief. The core distinction is between direct attention evidence and adjacent restoration or affect-only work.
questions.panel_notes: Starter constitution only. Other question-bank items still require drafting and review.
questions.source_question: Does exposure to natural environments improve directed attention in adults?
